coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for tumor marker detection based on nanoporous sliver@carbon dots as labels
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Ludan
Li, Meng
Zhang, Meng
Yan, Mei
Ge, Shenguang
Yu, Jinghua
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for detection of prostate protein antigen (PSA) was developed. The three-dimensional (3D) graphene conjugated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (3D-GR@AuNPs) was used to modify glassy carbon electrode (GCE), which provided an effective matrix for antibody immobilization. The 3D-composites were synthesized through freeze drying process and provided larger surface area compared with the conventional 2D structure. The carbon dots (CDs) simply prepared by electrooxidation of graphite was used as ECL reagent. The nanoporous sliver (NPS) with controllable 3D structure could provide a good platform for high loading of CDs, which formed the NPS@CDs composites as good ECL label. The good electrical conductivity of 3D-GR@AuNPs and high loading of CDs of NPS@CDs composites provided the significant advantage of dual-signal amplification technique. The new biosensor displayed good analytical performance for the detection of PSA in the range from 1pgmL−1 to 50ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.5pgmL−1. The sensor showed good reproducibility, acceptable precision and reliable stability, thus could provide many potential applications for the detection of tumor maker in the clinical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tumor markers
Three-dimensional graphene@gold nanoparticle composite
Nanoporous sliver@carbon dots composite
Electrochemiluminescence immunosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of NPS@CDs composite labeled Ab2
Characterization of the CDs, NPS, GO and 3D-GR@AuNPs
EIS characterization of the PSA sensor
Electrochemical and ECL behaviors of the NPS@CDs composite
Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the immunosensor
Application in detection of human serum samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication process of the PSA immunosensor.

(A) Representative TEM image of CDs and SAED pattern (inset) of CDs. (B) SEM image of NPS. (C) TEM image of GO. (D) SEM image of the 3D-GR@AuNPs and the magnification SEM (inset) image of 3D-GR@AuNPs.

EIS of the different electrodes: (a) bare GCE, (b) 3D-GR@AuNPs/GCE, (c) Ab1/3D-GR@AuNPs/GCE, (d) BSA/Ab1/3D-GR@AuNPs, (e) PSA/BSA/Ab1/3D-GR@AuNPs/GR (f) and NPS@CDs-Ab2/PSA/BSA/Ab1/3D-GR@AuNPs/GR in 0.1molL−1 KCl containing 5mmolL−1 [Fe(CN)6]−3/−4. The frequency range is between 0.01 and 100,000Hz with signal amplitude of 5mV.

ECL-potential curves of (a) pure CDs labeled-Ab2 and (b) NPS@CDs labeled-Ab2 (in 0.01molL−1 pH 7.4 PBS containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8). The concentration of PSA was 0.1ngmL−1. Scan rate: 0.1Vs−1. The voltage of photomultiplier tube was 800V. Inset is the ECL responses of the biosensor with the variously amplification label toward different target PSA concentrations: (a) pure CDs labeled-Ab2 and (b) NPS@CDs labeled-Ab2.

(A) The ECL intensity of 0.1ngmL−1 PSA with different incubation time for capturing PSA. (B) The ECL intensity of 0.1ngmL−1 PSA with different incubation time for recognizing NPS@CDs-Ab2. (C) The ECL intensity of 0.1ngmL−1 PSA with different PBS. (D) The ECL intensity of 0.1ngmL−1 PSA with different concentration of K2S2O8.

ECL profiles of the immunosensor in the presence (a–i) of different concentration of PSA and PSA concentration (ngmL−1) was (a) 0.001, (b) 0.003, (c) 0.01, (d) 0.05, (e) 0.1, (f) 0.3, (g) 0.5, (h) 1.0, (i) 5.0 (the voltage of the photomultiplier tube was at 800V). Inset is the calibration curve of the immunosensor.
The results of comparing with other immunoassay biosensing systems.

row.entry: CdTe quantum dots coated silica nanospheres

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of PSA determinations on human serum samples by the ECL immunosensor and ELISA.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of five successive determinations.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for tumor marker detection based on nanoporous sliver@carbon dots as labels

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Sensing & Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) immunosensor for detection of prostate protein antigen (PSA) was developed. The three-dimensional (3D) graphene conjugated with gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (3D-GR@AuNPs) was used to modify glassy carbon electrode (GCE), which provided an effective matrix for antibody immobilization. The 3D-composites were synthesized through freeze drying process and provided larger surface area compared with the conventional 2D structure. The carbon dots (CDs) simply prepared by electrooxidation of graphite was used as ECL reagent. The nanoporous sliver (NPS) with controllable 3D structure could provide a good platform for high loading of CDs, which formed the NPS@CDs composites as good ECL label. The good electrical conductivity of 3D-GR@AuNPs and high loading of CDs of NPS@CDs composites provided the significant advantage of dual-signal amplification technique. The new biosensor displayed good analytical performance for the detection of PSA in the range from 1pgmL−1 to 50ngmL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.5pgmL−1. The sensor showed good reproducibility, acceptable precision and reliable stability, thus could provide many potential applications for the detection of tumor maker in the clinical research.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, cancer is a complex disease which greatly affects the human health, for example, prostate cancer is one of the most commonly diagnosed malignancy and the sixth leading cause of cancer mortality afflicting the male population [1–3], consequently, early assessment of the tumor markers is critical for many biomedical research and diagnostics. Previously established detection means for prostate protein antigen (PSA) include the conventional enzyme linked immunosorbent assay, fluorescence [4], electrochemistry [5], surface plasmon resonance [6], chromatography [7], and surface enhanced Raman scattering techniques [8] and so on. However, there is still a critical demand for simple, rapid, sensitive and low-cost detection for the earlier and sensitive profiling of cancer biomarkers, for clinical and point-of-care applications. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), combining the electrochemistry with luminescence [9], involves the generation of species at the surface of an electrode that then undergo electron transfer reactions to form excited states and produce light [10–12]. ECL has been used as an important and powerful analytical tool and applied in many fields because of its potential-controlled property, simple instrumentation, low cost, high sensitivity, wide dynamic concentration response range [13–16].

Graphene (GR), a single layer of sp2 carbon atoms in a closely packed honeycomb two-dimensional (2D) lattice [17,18], has recently attracted enormous attention in constructing electrochemical biosensors due to its novel properties such as good mechanical strength, zero band gap, high carrier mobility, large specific surface area and outstanding electric conductivity [19,20]. In this work, three-dimensional (3D) graphene was synthesized to attach more gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) on surface and inside of the graphene, and improved the electronic transmission compared with 2D graphene. Therefore, the negative AuNPs attached on the surface of 3D-GR formed the 3D-GR@AuNPs hybrid architecture which can enhance electric ability and attach more antibodies. The nanoporous silver (NPS) is bicontinuous porous nanostructure with uniform and tunable pore and ligament dimensions of a few tens of nanometers and gained more and more interesting in a variety of areas [21–24]. The NPS with a controllable 3D structure express more easily connection properties and good electroconductibility. These NPS materials have been used for electrochemical sensors [25] with good conductivity and large surface area. Among those semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) is a fascinating class of environmentally friendly materials compared with the other ECL regents such as CdSe and CdTe. And CDs has recently attracted considerable attention due to its unique quantum effects, well biocompatibility, good chemical stability and excellent water solubility [26]. Moreover, there are amounts of easily functionalized carboxyl groups on its surface so that it can be easily for ECL labeling [27]. Due to the high surface area of NPS, NPS@CDs composites could link with abundant CDs so as to achieve the signal amplification technique. Herein, to enhance the immunosensor sensitivity, a new strategy using the NPS@CDs nanoparticles labeled secondary antibody to amplify the response of antigen–antibody sensing processes was proposed.

In the design and fabrication of highly sensitive ECL immunosensors, signal amplification and antibody immobilization are the crucial steps [28]. In this work, we have successfully designed a sensitive sandwich-type ECL immunosensor for PSA detection through self-assembly technique. The 3D-GR@AuNPs was used to modify the GCE to attach more antibodies and enhance the electron transfer so as to achieve signal amplification. Due to the hollow nanostructures of NPS, it has large enough area to attach abundant second antibodies on one hand, on the other, the NPS@CDs composites with high loading of CDs exhibit good ECL response, electric conductivity and biocompatibility. Due to the dual-signal amplification technique, a sensitive sandwich-type ECL immunosensor based on 3D-GR@AuNPs hybrids modified the GCE and NPS@CDs composite labeled antibody for PSA detection was constructed successfully, with a wide linear range, high sensitivity, and a low detection limit.

PSA, the primary anti-PSA (Ab1) and the secondary anti-PSA (Ab2) were gotten from Linc-Bio Science Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%) was obtained from Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). BSA, (96–99%) was obtained from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) was purchased from Nektar (Huntsville, AL). Gold chloride (HAuCl4) was bought from Alfa Aesar China Ltd. The natural graphite powder was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd. All chemicals and solvents used were of analytical grade and were used as received. Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments was purified through a Millipore water-purification system (≥18MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore). Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS, pH 7.4) were prepared using 0.01molL−1 Na2HPO4 and 0.01molL−1 KH2PO4. PBS (0.01molL−1, pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 and 0.1molL−1 KCl was used as the electrolyte. The PSA was stored at 4°C, and its standard solution was prepared daily with water as in use.

The ECL measurements were carried out on a MPI-E multifunctional electrochemical and chemiluminescence analytical system (Xi’an Remex Analytical Instrument Ltd. Co.) biased at 800V. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained from a Hitachi H-800 microscope (Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a JEOL-JSM-6300 scanning electron microscope. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns were recorded with a JEOL3000F microscope operating at 300keV, equipped with a field emission gun. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out on an IM6x electrochemical station (Zahner, Germany). All experiments were carried out with a conventional three-electrode system with the modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3mm in diameter) as the working electrode (WE), a platinum counter electrode (CE) and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) reference electrode (RE).

GO sheets were prepared from natural graphite powder through a modified Hummer′s method [29–31]. Graphite powder (15g) was put into H3PO4/H2SO4 (200mL, v:v=1:9), and then was stirred at room temperature. After KMnO4 (9.0g) was gradually added, the mixture were kept at 50°C under reflux and stirred for 12h, and then cooled to room temperature and poured into 500mL ice-water containing 8mL 30% H2O2. After the unexploited graphite in the resulting mixture was removed by centrifugation, the resulting GO was dehydration in 80°C under vacuum drying. The as-prepared GO was dispersed in ultrapure water at a concentration of 0.5mgmL−1 with the aid of ultrasound for further use.

PEG was used as reducing agent in the synthetic process to obtained 3D hybrids. Then 10mL GO (0.5mgmL−1), 200μL HAuCl4 (1%, w/w) and 20μL PEG were mixed uniformity in an ultrasonic bath for 1h, and then reacted in 180°C for 12h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was washed three times with water. Finally, 5mL mixture solution was got through freeze drying process [32] to obtain the 3D-GR@AuNPs composite. The graphene structure of a few microns in diameter was obtained after formation of a columnar ice phase and subsequent drying procedure and characterized by SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of NPS@CDs composite labeled Ab2
ce:section.ce:para: In brief, the NPS was prepared by dealloying the as-prepared Ag23Al77 alloys in 1.0molL−1 NaOH aqueous for 72h at 30°C [33], followed by rinsing with water for several times. These hollow nanostructures have particular advantages of highly accessible surface areas, and rich surface chemistry to allow for further functionalization. CDs was prepared in an electrochemical cell in pH 7.0 PBS consisting of a graphite rod WE, a Pt mesh CE, an Ag/AgCl RE. The applied potential at the graphite rod electrode was cycled between −3.0 and 3.0V at 0.1Vs−1, and ECL and electrochemical signals were measured. Carbon structure of the CDs ensures it to be an ideal candidate as nanosupports for biomolecules immobilization and biosensor fabrication. Then, 15mg NPS was mixed with the solution of 1mL CDs, and sonicated for 30min, then CDs could be attached on the surface of NPS due to the affinity of the NPS and carboxyl, eventually the NPS@CDs composite was obtained. Then the NPS@CDs composites solution was added with 1mL Ab2 (50μgmL−1) and sonicated for 30min. Unbound antibodies were removed by successive centrifugation and washed with water for three times. As a result the final composite solution NPS@CDs-Ab2 was obtained.

The process for preparing the PSA biosensor was shown schematically in Scheme 1 . Prior to ECL measurements, GCE electrode with 3mm diameter was polished carefully with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05μm Al2O3 powder on fine abrasive paper followed by rinsing thoroughly with water. Before modification, the bare electrode was scanned in 0.5molL−1 H2SO4 between −2 and 1V until a reproducible cyclic voltammogram (CV) was obtained. After successive sonication in 1:1 sulfuric acid, the electrode was rinsed with water and allowed to dry at room temperature. Onto the clean electrode, 5μL 3D-GR@AuNPs solution was added on and allowed to dry at room temperature. Then 5μL 50μgmL−1 Ab1 was added onto the electrode surface, after 20min of incubation, the electrode was washed with pH 7.4 PBS, following by dropping 1wt% BSA solution for 1h incubation to eliminate non-specific binding between the antigen and the electrode surface. Subsequently, the electrode was incubated with a varying concentration of PSA buffer solution, followed by washing extensively to remove unbounded antigen molecules. Finally, the prepared NPS@CDs-Ab2 solution was dropped onto the electrode surface. After another incubation of 25min, the electrode was washed for three times and ready for measurement.

Electrochemical measurements were done at room temperature and the potential swept from −1.8 to −0.5V with scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. The modified electrode above was in contact with the solution 0.01molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 and 0.1molL−1 KCl with a photomultiplier tube voltage of 800V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs, NPS, GO and 3D-GR@AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to many unique and novel characters of the carbon nanostructures, the strong and tunable luminescence of carbon materials further enhances their versatile properties. When the CDs banded with NPS, the effectiveness of ECL properties was increased significantly. Fig. 1A showed the TEM characterization of the CDs nanoparticles, as shown in the picture, most of the particles are small and spherical. The average size of CDs is about 4nm. The SAED pattern in the inset of Fig. 1A showed the good crystalline structure of CDs. Fig. 1B showed SEM images of NPS nanoparticles. The porous structure could be seen clearly and we can get knowledge from the picture that it has a bicontinuous and conductive structure, which is beneficial to the electrochemical applications. The ECL properties have great with the surface morphology of the modified WE, Fig. 1C showed TEM image of the 2D GO, as shown in the picture, there is a view of graphene oxide nanosheets that distinctly illustrating typical 2D flake-like wrinkled shapes of graphene with irregular size. Fig. 1D showed the SEM image of the 3D-GR@AuNPs hybrids with obvious 3D network structures. It is seen that the 3D-GR has been decorated with a large amount of Au nanoparticles about several nanometres. The effective surface area can be easily produced after 3D-GR@AuNPs was used as WE modifier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of the PSA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, EIS was an effective probing mean to get the knowledge of the surface features of modified electrodes to understand the chemical transformations and processes associated with the conductive electrode surface. The impedance spectra include a semicircle portion and a linear portion. The semicircle diameter at higher frequencies corresponds to the electron-transfer resistance (R et), and the linear part at lower frequencies corresponds to the diffusion process. The stepwise construction process of the immunosensor was characterized by impedance spectra as shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in the picture, the bare GCE (curve a) exhibiting a small semicircle in the spectrum which was characteristic of a mass diffusion limiting process. After the 3D-GR@AuNPs was coated onto the electrode, the semiconductor film decreased the impedance showed a much lower resistance for the redox probe (curve b). Subsequently when the 3D-GR@AuNPs modified electrode was coated by the Ab1 the R et increased which suggested that the Ab1 formed an additional barrier and further prevented the redox probe to the electrode surface (curve c). As the BSA could block non-specific sites of electrode surface, the R et increased significantly (curve d). Similarly, the R et increased after bounded with Ag (curve e) as the result of resisting the electron-transfer, then increased again after incubated with Ab2 (curve f), this is attributed to fact that the immobilization of immunocomplex substances was effective.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical and ECL behaviors of the NPS@CDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 displays ECL-potential waves to evaluate the intensity of ECL properties at different modified electrode interfaces. The ECL electrodes were scanned from −0.5 to −1.8V in 0.01molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 and 0.1molL−1 KCl. The ECL behavior of NPS@CDs was confirmed to involve the formation of an excited-state CDs (R*) via electron-transfer annihilation of positively charged (R*+) CDs and negatively charged (R*−) CDs. Additionally, the excellent ECL response of CDs could be obtained by using S2O8 2− as a coreactant, suggesting that electron-transfer annihilation of R*− and the strongly oxidizing SO4 *− radicals produced by electroreduction of S2O8 2− might be involved in CDs-S2O8 2− ECL processes [34]. The curve a in Fig. 3 showed ECL-potential curves of pure CDs-labeled Ab2 and curve b showed the ECL-potential curves of the NPS@CDs composite labeled Ab2. The quantity of the CDs and NPS@CDs nanoparticles were equal in both labels. As shown in the Fig. 3, the CDs-labeled Ab2 performers a weak ECL emission, whereas, the NPS@CDs composite labeled Ab2 reveals excellent ECL performance and signal amplification about to ten times compared with pure CDs nanoparticles. The reason may be that nano-NPS@CDs has a higher effective electrochemical surface area, good biocompatibility, and facilitated the ECL reaction. The inset shows plots of ECL intensity of the immunosensor vs. the PSA concentration of pure CDs (a) and NPS@CDs composites labeled Ab2 (b), respectively. Therefore, the NPS@CDs composite had highly effective ECL properties, which were promising for the construction of the ECL biosensor.
The effects of incubation time, the concentration of the K2S2O8 and pH value on the ECL intensity of the immunosensor were investigated as follows.

The effects of the incubation time of Ab1 and PSA and the incubation time of PSA and Ab2 were shown in Fig. 4 , the optimal conditions for different parameters were as follows: the incubation time of Ab1 and PSA was 25min (Fig. 4A), the incubation time of PSA and Ab2 was 25min (Fig. 4B).

The study of pH influence on the ECL detection was conducted in the range of 6.0–9.0 as shown in Fig. 4C. The ECL intensity was increased with the increase of pH value from 6.0 to 7.4 and then decreased. The result may be leaded the fact that the high acidic or alkaline surroundings would damage the immobilized protein [35]. Since the optimal pH value for the biological systems was 7.4, the ECL detection was performed in pH 7.4 PBS.

In addition, the effect of the concentration of the K2S2O8 in the 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4) was shown in the Fig. 4D, with the increase of concentration, the ECL intensity increased, and then reached a significantly amplification when the concentration of the K2S2O8 was 0.1molL−1. Therefore the optimal ECL response was achieved at the concentration of K2S2O8 0.1molL−1.

Under the optimal conditions, Fig. 5 shows the ECL profiles of the immunosensor after incubating in different concentrations of PSA. As can be seen in Fig. 5, the ECL intensity increased linearly with the concentration of PSA over the range from 0.001 to 50ngmL−1 and the regression equation was I ECL =6968.3+2136.5lg c PSA(cPSA/ngmL−1) with a correlation coefficient was 0.9979 (n =6). The limit of detection for PSA was 0.5pgmL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. The relative standard deviation of 11 replicate determinations of PSA was 3.0, 2.6 and 2.3% at 0.01, 0.1 and 10ngmL−1 (n =11), respectively. The results demonstrated that the proposed method could be used for the determination of PSA. Moreover, Table 1 shows the linear range and detection limit of immunosensors with previous reports. The limit of detection was much lower than those of 0.00072–100ngmL−1 for the reported competitive immunosensors (Table 1) [36–41]. The NPG@CDs composite as an ECL label with amplification technique greatly increased the sensitivity and extended the detectable concentration range. Therefore compared with other methods, the immunosensor has a relatively large linear range and low detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability, and reproducibility of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the immunosensor is very important in analyzing biological samples without separation. To characterize the specificity of the immunosensor, 0.1ngmL−1 PSA containing 100ngmL−1 human chorionic gonadotrophin (HCG) human, 100ngmL−1 carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and 100ngmL−1 alpha fetoprotein (AFP) and a same solution containing additional interfere of 200ngmL−1 was measured by the immunosensor. The ECL intensity changes were not significant compared with 1.0ngmL−1 pure PSA, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was 5.6%. The result indicated that the specificity of the immunosensor for the determination of PSA was acceptable.

The immunosensor was stored in pH 7.4 PBS in the refrigerator at 4°C when not used for 4 weeks, then was used to detect the same PSA concentration, the ECL intensity of the immunosensor retained 90.0% of the initial response, which demonstrated that the stability of the immunosensor was quite good.

The reproducibility of the immunosensor was evaluated by both intra- and inter-assay precision. The intra-assay precision of the used method was assaying one PSA level for five replicate measurements while the inter-assay precision was determining one PSA level with five immunosensors made at the same electrode. The intra- and inter-assay variation coefficients (VCs) obtained from 1ngmL−1 PSA were 6.2 and 7.1%, respectively. Obviously, the inter-assay VC showed a good electrode-to-electrode reproducibility of the fabrication protocol, while the low value of intra-assay VC indicated that the immunosensor could be regenerated and used repeatedly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in detection of human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Several clinical serum samples were assayed to validate the feasibility of the immunoassay system for clinical applications. These serum samples were diluted to different concentrations with PBS of pH 7.4, and the results were then compared with the reference values obtained by the conventional enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) method (Table 2 ). The relative deviations were 3.31, 3.83, 4.03, 3.16, and 2.47%, respectively. It obviously expresses that there is no significant difference between the results and ELISA method. Thus, the developed immunosensor could be successfully applied to the clinical determination of PSA levels in human serum.

In this work, a novel ECL immunosensor for a rapid and sensitive detection of PSA was first constructed based on 3D-GR@AuNPs modified electrode and NPS@CDs as ECL label. An ultrasensitive sandwich ECL immunoassay method for the detection of PSA with high sensitivity and selectivity was successfully realized. The 3D-GR@AuNPs composite could be an ideal substrate for antibody immobilization with good stability and bioactivity. The NPS with uniform pore size, large surface area and good biocompatibility can be simply prepared by a dealloying method. The immunosensor in this paper used functionalized NPS@CDs composite as an ideal label and ECL reagent, which has excellent labeling property and ECL activity with amplification techniques. The proposed ECL immunosensor showed excellent analytical performance for the detecation of PSA with convenient operability, high sensitivity, and acceptable reproducibility, stability, and accuracy, therefore, the as-proposed immunosensor provides great promise in clinical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Research Foundation of China (2127705, 21175058), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2012BZ002).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ludan Wu studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Meng Li studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.
Meng Zhang studies in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Mei Yan received her BSc in applied chemistry from University of Jinan in 1999, and obtained her PhD in 2005 from Institute of chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences. She then joined University of Jinan, as an associate professor working on the synthesis and performance of advanced functional materials.

Shenguang Ge is a PhD student at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering in Shandong University, completed his master degree studies in University of Jinan in 2006. He joined University of Jinan, where currently he is a lecturer of chemistry. His research interests are in the area of biosensor and chemsensor.

Jinghua Yu received her PhD degree in analytical chemistry in 2003 from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, China. She is currently positioned as a professor at University of Jinan. She spends most of her time investigating biomedical engineering, especially for the development of biosensor devices and analytical tools.
